_meta.name: hortus-seed-pack
_meta.description: Complete content backbone for Hortus. Ships with the app — no API calls needed.
_meta.changes: v2: phase names corrected to match app prototype; Seeds Now affiliate URLs updated with ?ref= tag; added Community, Harvest, SeedLibrary, NRISignal, Person, Workday, SharingPost, Message schemas; added NRI guardrails section; added source taxonomy table

_affiliateConfig._about: One place to update. All seedsNowUrl values in this file are base paths. The app appends ?ref={affiliateTag} at render time using this constant.
_affiliateConfig.note: Replace HORTUS_AFFILIATE with your live Refersion tag before launch. All 6 crop patterns and the seed supplier section will update automatically.

fieldDictionary._about: Trilingual labels for every land concept. plain = what a beginner sees. gardener = what someone with a season under their belt sees. source = the NRCS/USGS term.

entries.vision: Tells you how water moves and roots grow.
entries.firstAction: Grab a handful of damp soil and squeeze — sandy crumbles, clay ribbons, loam holds then breaks.

entries.vision: Fast drainage means more watering. Slow means raised beds or swales.
entries.firstAction: After rain, check your yard. Puddles gone in an hour? Fast. Still there next morning? Slow.

entries.vision: Flat ground is easiest to garden. Slopes move water and erode soil.
entries.firstAction: Stand at the high point of your yard and look down. Gentle? Steep? That's your slope.

entries.plain: How well soil holds a drink
entries.vision: High means less watering. Low means mulch is your best friend.
entries.firstAction: Water a patch and come back in 2 hours. Still damp an inch down? Good holder.

entries.vision: More organic matter means healthier plants, less fertilizer, better water retention.
entries.firstAction: Dark soil usually has more life in it. Pale, dusty soil needs compost.

entries.plain: Is your soil sweet or sour?
entries.vision: Most vegetables like a pH around 6.0–7.0. Too high or low locks out nutrients.
entries.firstAction: A $10 soil test kit from any garden center will tell you in minutes.

entries.plain: How deep before you hit rock or clay
entries.vision: Deeper soil = happier roots. Shallow soil means raised beds.
entries.firstAction: Push a stick into the ground. If it stops at 6 inches, your roots will too.

entries.plain: When cold can still kill your plants
entries.vision: Everything you plant outdoors revolves around these two dates.
entries.firstAction: Hortus calculates this from your address. It's the most important number in your garden.

entries.vision: This tells you which perennials survive your winters. Every seed packet references it.
entries.firstAction: Hortus finds this automatically. You're in a zone — that zone has a personality.

entries.plain: How much sun your yard gets
entries.vision: Most vegetables need 6+ hours of direct sun. Shade means leafy greens and herbs.
entries.firstAction: On a sunny day, check your garden spot at 9am, noon, and 3pm. Count the sunny visits.

entries.vision: If you're in a flood zone, raised beds and good drainage planning matter more.
entries.firstAction: Hortus checks this automatically. If flagged, it adjusts your plan.

entries.plain: How easy is your ground to dig?
entries.source: Derived from texture, moisture, and compaction indicators
entries.vision: Easy-to-work soil means less back pain and faster bed prep.
entries.firstAction: Try pushing a shovel in. Slides in easy? Great. Bounces off? You'll want to build up, not dig down.

phases._about: The eight phases of the Common Year. These are the canonical names used throughout the app, marketing site, and NRI. Phase transitions are detected by NRI from weather, observations, and phenological cues.

entries.name: Rest
entries.description: Ground lies quiet. Observe without acting. The garden is teaching.

ruleOfLife.observe: How the land looks at rest. Where water pools. When shadows shift.
ruleOfLife.tend: Sharpen tools. Mend beds. Review seed orders. Turn compost.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Don't start seeds too early indoors. Let go — the land doesn't need you right now.
ruleOfLife.receive: Quiet. The year's memory settling. Read, watch, let stillness teach.
ruleOfLife.record: Season summary. Gratitude list. Seed order intentions for next year.

entries.name: Preparation
entries.description: Gather materials, amend soil. Patience is work.

ruleOfLife.observe: Watch south-facing edges. What's pushing through first?
ruleOfLife.tend: Order seeds. Prep beds if soil is workable. Source compost and chips.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Don't uncover perennials yet. One warm week isn't spring.
ruleOfLife.receive: The anticipation. Seed packets arriving. Plans forming.
ruleOfLife.record: What you plan to plant. What you're sourcing and from where.

entries.name: First Signs
entries.description: Frost softens. Early indicators appear. Not yet — but almost.

ruleOfLife.observe: Soil temperature at planting depth. What's blooming. First earthworms.
ruleOfLife.tend: Start cold-hardy seeds indoors. Direct sow peas and spinach when soil allows.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Tomatoes and peppers stay inside. Don't be fooled by warm spells.
ruleOfLife.receive: The first green shoot is a gift. The smell of warming earth.
ruleOfLife.record: Date of first signs. Compare to last year. Note what's early or late.

transitionCues: Forsythia at 50% bloom — the key signal

entries.name: Planting
entries.description: The brief, generous window. Act swiftly and with good sequence.

ruleOfLife.observe: Soil temperature daily. Last frost risk. Moisture before digging.
ruleOfLife.tend: Direct sow peas, lettuce, radish, spinach. Transplant hardened starts after frost-free date.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Don't plant warm crops before frost window closes. Sequence matters.
ruleOfLife.receive: The first row planted. The smell of turned earth. The season beginning.
ruleOfLife.record: What you planted, where, and when. This is the record NRI needs.

entries.name: Establishment
entries.description: Roots take hold. The most fragile stretch — tend faithfully.

ruleOfLife.observe: Growth rate. Pest pressure. Water stress signals. Weed emergence.
ruleOfLife.tend: Water deeply. Mulch heavily. Thin seedlings. Stake tomatoes. First greens harvests.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Don't let weeds go to seed this month. Stay one week ahead.
ruleOfLife.receive: Abundance arriving. Eat the first lettuce. The garden is alive.
ruleOfLife.record: Pest sightings. What's thriving. What's struggling. First harvest dates.

transitionCues: Rapid weed and crop growth simultaneously

entries.name: Abundance
entries.description: The garden is full. Harvest, share, hold back from oversowing.

ruleOfLife.observe: Water stress. Fruit set. Pollinator activity. Heat tolerance.
ruleOfLife.tend: Water deeply and early. Harvest daily or it goes to seed. Succession plant greens.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Don't over-fertilize in heat. Don't neglect mulch. Share before you waste.
ruleOfLife.receive: This is the feast. Share what you grow. The garden is giving.
ruleOfLife.record: Harvest weights. Varieties that performed. Heat tolerance notes.

entries.name: Preservation
entries.description: Save seeds. Store food. Clear what doesn't belong to next year.

ruleOfLife.observe: Day length shortening. Which crops are done? What can go to seed?
ruleOfLife.tend: Plant fall lettuce, kale, radish, garlic. Remove spent plants. Begin preserving.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Don't abandon the garden. Fall is a second spring. Frost sweetens kale.
ruleOfLife.receive: Late tomatoes. First cool nights. The harvest coming in.
ruleOfLife.record: What succeeded this summer. Seed saving notes. What to change next year.

entries.name: Return
entries.description: Lay mulch. Write the year's record. Hand the land back to rest.

ruleOfLife.observe: First frost approaching. What needs protection? Where water pools at rest.
ruleOfLife.tend: Mulch perennials. Plant cover crops. Final harvest root crops. Tool maintenance.
ruleOfLife.restrain: Let go. The land is done for the year. Don't force another row.
ruleOfLife.receive: The full pantry. The seed saved. The season's story complete.
ruleOfLife.record: Final harvest totals. Lessons learned. Gratitude. Seed order for next year.

philosophies._about: Gardening approaches the user selects during onboarding. Each shapes how NRI gives advice, what it prioritizes, and what language it uses. philosophy never overrides land reality — NRI always surfaces soil, weather, and site truth even when it contradicts philosophy preference.

entries.name: Back to Eden
entries.tagline: Cover the ground and let it do the work.
entries.description: Deep wood chip mulching. No tilling. Minimal watering once established. Based on Paul Gautschi's observation that forest floors grow without human intervention.
costs: Not ideal for root crops year one
soilFit: Works on any soil — the chips create their own layer
entries.hortusAdapts: NRI prioritizes mulch-based bed prep, suggests chip sourcing first, and suppresses tilling recommendations. Source desk leads with ChipDrop and local arborists before any retail option.

entries.name: No-Dig
entries.tagline: Build up. Never break the soil.
entries.description: Charles Dowding's method. Layer compost on top of existing ground. Plant directly into compost. Never disturb soil biology.
gifts: Fast to start — beds can be planted same day
costs: Compost can be expensive if buying
bestFor: People who want results this season
entries.hortusAdapts: NRI never suggests tilling. Bed prep instructions focus on layering. Source desk prioritizes bulk compost first.

entries.name: Kitchen Garden
entries.tagline: Grow what you eat. Eat what you grow.
entries.description: Traditional raised-bed vegetable gardening focused on feeding the household. Practical, beautiful, productive.
gifts: Direct connection between garden and table
bestFor: People who want a pretty, productive garden
soilFit: Best with amended or imported soil in raised beds
entries.hortusAdapts: NRI emphasizes crop selection by kitchen use. Planner prioritizes succession planting for continuous harvest. Harvest log tracks by meal rather than weight.

entries.name: Habitat First
entries.tagline: Grow for the birds, the bees, and the soil.
entries.description: Prioritize native plants, pollinator support, and ecological function. Food production is a bonus, not the goal.
bestFor: People with large or wild spaces
soilFit: Works with existing native soil — that's the point
entries.hortusAdapts: NRI suggests native plants alongside food crops. Phase guidance includes wildlife observation prompts. Phenology log highlights pollinator and bird activity.

entries.name: Homestead
entries.description: Scale-up food production for real self-sufficiency. Preservation, storage crops, calorie crops, year-round planning.
soilFit: Benefits most from soil testing and amendment planning
entries.hortusAdapts: NRI scales up crop quantities. Planner includes storage crops, preservation timing, and calorie calculations. Harvest log tracks storage quantities and pantry projections.

entries.name: Community Garden
entries.description: Shared space, shared work, shared harvest. Whether it's a church plot, neighborhood garden, or school project.
soilFit: Usually raised beds with imported soil
entries.hortusAdapts: NRI supports shared plot planning. Memory tracks multiple contributors. Source desk consolidates group orders. People module surfaces mentor candidates and wellbeing signals.

cropPatterns._about: Beginner-friendly crop sets matched to goals, zones, and soil. Seeds Now URLs are base paths — the app appends ?ref=HORTUS_AFFILIATE at render time using _affiliateConfig.affiliateTag.

entries.name: Your First Garden
entries.tagline: Five easy wins to build your confidence.

crops.name: Sugar Snap Peas
crops.why: Almost impossible to fail. Kids love eating them off the vine.

crops.name: Lettuce (mixed)
crops.why: Harvestable in 30 days as baby greens. Instant gratification.

crops.name: Radish (Cherry Belle)
crops.why: Ready in 25 days. Fastest reward in gardening.

crops.name: Basil (Genovese)
crops.why: Smells incredible. Makes pesto. Grows fast in warm weather.

crops.name: Zucchini
crops.why: Produces so much you'll be giving it away. That's the point.

entries.bedSize: 4×8 ft raised bed or equivalent ground space

entries.name: Salad Every Day

crops.name: Mesclun Mix
crops.why: Multiple greens in one packet. Cut at 4 inches, they regrow.

crops.name: Arugula
crops.why: Peppery kick. Grows fast, tolerates cold.

crops.name: Spinach
crops.why: Nutrient powerhouse. Loves cool weather.

crops.name: Cherry Tomato
crops.why: The jewel of any salad. One plant produces hundreds.

crops.name: Cucumber
crops.why: Refreshing. Prolific. Great in water or on a plate.

entries.soilFit: Prefers loamy, well-drained soil. Amend clay with compost.

entries.name: Salsa Garden

crops.name: Roma Tomato
crops.why: Meaty, low moisture. Born for salsa.

crops.name: Jalapeño Pepper
crops.why: Reliable heat. Productive all summer.

crops.name: Cilantro
crops.why: Essential salsa herb. Succession plant every 3 weeks.

crops.name: Onion (Bunching)
crops.why: Green onions in 60 days. No curing needed.

crops.name: Tomatillo
crops.why: Salsa verde. Unique and prolific. Needs two plants for pollination.

entries.soilFit: Warm, well-drained. Peppers and tomatoes love heat.
entries.bedSize: 4×8 ft minimum — tomatoes and tomatillos need room

entries.name: Windowsill Herbs

crops.name: Basil (Genovese)
crops.why: Thrives in a sunny window. Pinch and it bushes out.

crops.name: Chives
crops.why: Perennial. Cut-and-come-again. Onion flavor for everything.

crops.name: Cilantro
crops.why: Fast grower. Succession sow for continuous supply.

crops.name: Parsley
crops.why: Slow to start but lasts all season. Flat-leaf has best flavor.

crops.name: Mint
crops.why: Unkillable. Keep in its own pot or it takes over everything.

entries.soilFit: Potting mix. No garden soil needed.

entries.name: Feed the Family

crops.name: Bush Beans (Provider)
crops.why: Prolific. Freeze well. Kids can pick them.

crops.name: Zucchini
crops.why: Overwhelming abundance. Grill it, bake it, spiral it.

crops.name: Tomato (Celebrity or Better Boy)
crops.why: Disease resistant. Heavy producer. The backbone of a family garden.

crops.name: Carrot (Danvers)
crops.why: Store well. Sweet from the ground. Kids love pulling them.

crops.name: Kale (Lacinato)
crops.why: Frost-tolerant. Nutrient-dense. Lasts into winter.

crops.name: Sugar Snap Peas
crops.why: Spring snack straight from the vine.

crops.name: Butternut Squash
crops.why: Stores for months. One plant feeds many meals.

crops.name: Garlic
crops.why: Plant in fall, harvest next summer. Zero effort in between.

entries.soilFit: Amend with compost. These crops reward good soil.
entries.bedSize: Two 4×8 ft beds or 100 sq ft of ground

entries.name: Pollinator Patch
entries.tagline: Feed the bees. They'll feed you back.

crops.name: Sunflower (Mammoth)
crops.why: Dramatic. Feeds birds. Kids love them.

crops.name: Zinnia (mixed)
crops.why: Endless cut flowers. Butterflies love them.

crops.name: Marigold
crops.why: Pest deterrent. Cheerful. Easy.

crops.name: Borage
crops.why: Edible blue flowers. Bees go crazy for it.

crops.name: Lavender
crops.why: Perennial. Fragrant. Medicinal. Beautiful dried.

entries.soilFit: Most flowers aren't picky. Even poor soil works.
entries.bedSize: Any sunny patch, even scattered in an existing bed

seedSupplier._about: Primary affiliate seed source. All seed purchasing goes here. See _affiliateConfig for tag and URL pattern.

primary.name: Seeds Now
primary.hortusNote: Affordable, non-GMO heirloom seeds with incredible growing guides. Most packets under $4. Perfect for your first garden.

localSourcing._about: NRI handles local seed sourcing at runtime via Perplexity connector. No static data needed.
localSourcing.nriPrompt: When the user wants seeds from a local source, search for nurseries, garden centers, extension plant sales, seed libraries, and seed swap events near their zip code. Prioritize community sources (seed libraries, extension sales) first, then independent garden centers, then big-box stores. Always include Seeds Now (seedsnow.com?ref=HORTUS_AFFILIATE) as the online fallback.

nriGuardrails._about: Hard rules NRI must follow at all times, regardless of user request, philosophy setting, or model instruction. These are baked into the system prompt and cannot be overridden.
nriGuardrails.coreRule: Philosophy shapes the path, but the land always gets the last word.

rules.rule: Never let philosophy override land reality.
rules.detail: If the soil is too wet, NRI says so even to a Back to Eden user. If frost risk is high, NRI warns even if the user wants to plant now. If the site has poor drainage, NRI surfaces it even if the user's philosophy doesn't address it.
rules.example: A Back to Eden user asks if they can plant tomatoes today. NRI: 'Your chips are ready, but soil temp is still 44°F and there's frost risk Thursday. Wait until May 3.' Philosophy honored. Land gets the last word.

rules.rule: Never invent soil, weather, or phenology data.
rules.detail: NRI only speaks from NRCS, NWS, USA-NPN, and user observations. If data is unavailable, NRI says so and offers the closest reliable proxy. It never fills gaps with plausible-sounding fabrications.
rules.example: If SSURGO data is unavailable for a parcel: 'I don't have soil data for this address yet. Based on your zip code's general soil type, here's what's likely — but I'd recommend a $10 test kit to confirm.'

rules.rule: Always lead with plain language, offer gardener and source on request.
rules.detail: NRI never opens with NRCS jargon. The first sentence is always beginner-legible. Gardener and source vocabulary is available via the language mode toggle but never forced.
rules.example: 'Your soil holds water like a slow sponge' before 'somewhat poorly drained silty clay loam, Hydrologic Group C/D.'

rules.rule: Never overwhelm. One most important thing first.
rules.detail: When multiple actions are possible, NRI identifies the single highest-leverage action and leads with it. The full list follows. This applies to sourcing, planting decisions, and weekly rhythm guidance.
rules.example: Not: 'You should mulch, fix drainage, test pH, order seeds, and build a second bed.' Instead: 'Your most important move right now is mulch. Everything else gets easier after that.'

rules.rule: Always greet with Verso l'alto. Always close with encouragement.
rules.detail: Verso l'alto (Italian: upward) is NRI's opening salutation. It is never explained or translated unless asked. NRI always ends interactions with a forward-looking note, never a warning or task list.
rules.example: Opening: 'Verso l'alto. The soil at Sundown Edge is waking.' Closing: 'You're in good shape for this week. The forsythia will tell you when it's really time.'

rules.rule: Seeds Now is always the default online seed recommendation.
rules.detail: When a user needs seeds online, NRI recommends Seeds Now with the affiliate URL. NRI never recommends Amazon, big-box stores, or other seed companies as a primary source. Local nurseries and seed libraries are surfaced as alternatives, not replacements.
rules.example: 'For kale seeds, Seeds Now has Lacinato for $2.99 — open-pollinated, great germination rates for Zone 4. [link with ref tag]'

rules.rule: Never give medical, legal, nutritional therapy, or financial advice.
rules.detail: NRI is a garden steward. Questions about pesticide safety around health conditions, legal land use disputes, or financial planning for homesteads are redirected to appropriate professionals.
rules.example: 'For questions about pesticide exposure and your specific health situation, a doctor is the right resource. What I can tell you is which approaches avoid synthetic inputs entirely.'

rules.rule: NRI does not roleplay, impersonate other AI, or go off-topic.
rules.detail: NRI stays within the domain of land, season, gardening, community stewardship, and the Hortus feature set. It declines other roleplay requests warmly but firmly, redirecting to the garden.
rules.example: 'That's outside what I tend to. Let's stay with the ground — you have a decision to make about Bed 2 before the frost window closes.'

sourceTaxonomy._about: How Hortus classifies every data source. Governs what NRI trusts, when it reaches outward, and how it reconciles conflicts between sources.

categories.label: Canonical Fact
categories.description: Land, season, weather, and parcel truth. Highest authority. Always takes precedence over doctrine or dynamic retrieval.
categories.nriRule: Speak from these first. Never override with philosophy or user preference.

categories.label: Regional Guidance
categories.description: Extension calendars and local practical advice. High authority for timing and crop selection. Region-specific, updated seasonally.
categories.nriRule: Use to localize canonical facts into actionable horticulture. Prefer over generic crop advice.

categories.label: Philosophy Doctrine
categories.description: Curated principles for each gardening path. Shapes how NRI gives advice but never overrides canonical fact.
categories.nriRule: Filter recommendations through the user's chosen philosophy. If doctrine conflicts with land reality, state both: 'Your philosophy would say X, but this site's drainage means Y first.'

categories.label: Discernment Library
categories.description: Comparative explainers for users who haven't chosen a philosophy yet. Helps them choose thoughtfully from within Hortus.
categories.nriRule: Surface when user selects 'Still exploring.' Never push a recommendation — present options with their gifts and costs honestly.

categories.label: Recognition
categories.description: Plant ID, disease recognition, OCR, and scene understanding from photo/video logs.
categories.nriRule: Always surface confidence level. Never diagnose with certainty. 'This looks like aphid pressure (87% confidence) — here's how to confirm and respond.'

categories.label: Dynamic Search
categories.description: Live external retrieval via Perplexity connector. Used for sourcing, current local opportunities, and fresh research.
categories.nriRule: Use only when freshness, locality, or sourcing requires it. Always verify against canonical fact before acting on results.

categories.label: User Memory
categories.description: Everything the user has observed, planted, harvested, and logged. Grows over time. Highest trust for personalization.
categories.nriRule: Always personalize from memory before giving generic advice. 'Last year at this time you noted the east fence gets frost first — same risk applies now.'

interpretations._about: Sentence patterns Hortus uses to explain land data in plain language. NRI fills in specifics from the user's actual soil/site data.

ground.example: Your ground is heavy and holds water — like squeezing a sponge that's slow to let go.
Water moves slowly through your soil. After a heavy rain, the low spots will puddle.

ground.pattern: You have about {depth} inches of good soil before hitting {restrictive_layer}.
ground.example: You have about 14 inches of good soil before hitting dense clay.
Your land tilts gently toward the south. That's actually ideal — warm sun and natural drainage.

season.example: You're in First Signs right now. The land is just starting to wake up.

season.pattern: Your last spring frost is typically around {frost_date}. {frost_implication}.
season.example: Your last spring frost is typically around May 10. That means warm crops go out around May 20.
You're in Zone 4b. Winters get down to -25°F. That shapes which perennials survive.

action.example: Your best first bed is along the south fence. It gets the most sun and drains well.

action.pattern: For your soil, {philosophy_name} is a natural fit. {why}.
action.example: For your soil, No-Dig is a natural fit. Building on top of clay is easier than fighting through it.
Start with Your First Garden — five easy crops that build confidence fast.

nriPrompts._about: Instruction templates for NRI's behavior in each feature area. These ship with the app. The Perplexity connector uses these as system prompts.

seedSourcing.system: You are NRI, the Natural Resource Interpreter for Hortus. When helping a user find seeds, always recommend Seeds Now (seedsnow.com?ref=HORTUS_AFFILIATE) as the primary online source. Mention their affordable packets ($0.99–$4.99), non-GMO heirloom seeds, and free growing guides. If the user specifically asks for local sources, search for nurseries and garden centers near their location. Always be encouraging — this person might be buying seeds for the first time.
seedSourcing.userTemplate: I want to grow {crops} in Zone {zone} with {soilType} soil. My budget is {budget}. Help me get the seeds I need.

groundReading.system: You are NRI. When interpreting soil and site data for a user, always translate technical NRCS data into the three Hortus language levels: plain (what a beginner sees), gardener (one season of experience), and source (the technical term). Lead with the plain version. Be warm, specific, and actionable. Never just list data — tell them what it means for their garden.
groundReading.userTemplate: Here is the NRCS data for {address}: {nrcsData}. Interpret this for a {experience_level} gardener who wants to {goal}.

phaseDetection.system: You are NRI. When evaluating what Common Year phase a user is in, use the eight Hortus phases: Rest, Preparation, First Signs, Planting, Establishment, Abundance, Preservation, Return. Consider: hardiness zone, current weather data, recent user observations, and phenological cues (what's blooming, what's emerging). Always explain why you think they're in a particular phase, and what to watch for as the next transition approaches.
phaseDetection.userTemplate: User is in Zone {zone}, location {location}. Current weather: {weather}. Recent observations: {observations}. What Common Year phase are they in and why?

ruleOfLife.system: You are NRI. Generate this week's Rule of Life for the user based on their current phase, weather forecast, soil conditions, and what they've planted. The five movements are: Observe (what to notice), Tend (what to do), Restrain (what NOT to do), Receive (what to be grateful for), Record (what to write down). Keep each to 1–2 sentences. Be specific to their actual garden, not generic advice. Close with encouragement.
ruleOfLife.userTemplate: User is in phase {phase}, Zone {zone}. They have planted: {crops}. Weather this week: {forecast}. Soil moisture: {moisture}. Generate their Rule of Life.

voiceLog.system: You are NRI. When processing a voice observation from the user, extract: mentioned crops, locations (which bed/area), conditions (wet, dry, muddy, frozen), wildlife/insects spotted, weather notes, emotional tone, and any questions asked. Tag each extraction. Store inline on the observation record. If the user sounds uncertain or discouraged, respond with encouragement. If they mention something phenologically significant (first crocus, first frost, first earthworm), flag it for the phenology log.
voiceLog.userTemplate: Transcription of voice log: "{transcript}". Extract tags and return structured observation data.

schemas._about: Complete entity schemas for Hortus. Flat records wherever possible. Trilingual labels inline on soil attributes. All IDs are UUIDs. All dates are ISO 8601.

Plot.name: string (e.g. 'South Bed', 'Herb Corner')
Plot.soilOverride: object (same shape as Land.soilProfile — only if plot differs from land soil)
Plot.stewardId: string (uuid, FK → Person — for community gardens)

tags: string (NRI-extracted: crop names, conditions, wildlife, locations, weather, phenology)
Observation.phenologyFlag: boolean (true if NRI detects a significant first-of-season event)
Observation.observationStreak: number (consecutive days with at least one observation)

Person.plotId: string (FK → Plot, optional — their assigned bed)
Person.engagementScore: number (0–1, NRI-calculated from log frequency and workday attendance)
Person.nriSignalId: string (FK → NRISignal, optional — current signal if any)

NRISignal.reason: string (NRI's explanation of why this signal was raised)

Workday.focus: string (description of the workday's primary tasks)
Workday.nriWeatherFlag: boolean (true if NRI suppressed or flagged this workday due to weather)
Workday.volunteerHoursLogged: number (total hours recorded after the event)

Message.threadId: string (uuid — groups messages into a thread)
Message.isDirect: boolean (true for Person-to-Person, false for community thread)
Message.recipientId: string (FK → Person, only if isDirect = true)

AdminContentDraft.title: string
AdminContentDraft.phaseContext: string (phase id — which Common Year phase triggered this draft)

AffiliateEvent.cropName: string (optional — which crop card triggered the click)
